explanation: This formula means that whenever prop_0 becomes true, it must be the case that prop_1 held at some point strictly before that, and never again afterwards. In standard LTL, this is usually expressed as: until prop_0, prop_1 must hold at least once. Formally, 'Before prop_0, the controller shall prop_1' expresses that if prop_0 happens eventually, prop_1 must occur strictly before that point. This can be captured by: '(!prop_0) U (prop_1 & (!prop_0) U prop_0)', but is more simply and typically written as: '(!prop_0) -> (!prop_0 U prop_1)'. Nested U is a bit subtle, but the key idea is that if prop_0 ever happens, there must have been a prop_1 before.

This states that at all times before 'prop_0' becomes true, 'prop_1' must be true. Once 'prop_0' becomes true, we do not care about 'prop_1' anymore. This is typically encoded as: 'Before prop_0, always prop_1', which in LTL is: (!prop_0) -> (!prop_0 U prop_0), so we want to say 'globally, if not prop_0, then prop_1' UNTIL prop_0. In LTL, it's: 'not prop_0 U (prop_0 | (not prop_0 & prop_1))', but more simply: '!(prop_0) -> prop_1' globally until 'prop_0'. Thus: G( (!prop_0) -> prop_1 ) U prop_0.

The phrase implies that at all points before prop_0 becomes true, prop_1 must be true. This is captured by 'not prop_0' releases prop_1: (¬prop_0) V prop_1, or equivalently, globally if not prop_0, then prop_1: G(¬prop_0 -> prop_1).

The statement says that before 'prop_0' becomes true, 'prop_1' should be true. In LTL, this is often formalized as: if 'prop_0' has not occurred yet, then 'prop_1' must hold up to that point. This can be captured using the 'until' and 'globally' operators.

The specification states that before prop_0 becomes true, the controller shall satisfy prop_1. This means that at every point in the execution, if prop_0 has not occurred yet, then prop_1 must hold. In LTL, this can be expressed as: 'not prop_0' globally implies prop_1, i.e., as long as prop_0 hasn't happened, prop_1 must be true globally until prop_0.

The phrase 'Before prop_0, the prop_1' can be understood as: if prop_0 has not yet happened, then prop_1 must be true up until the first occurrence of prop_0. This translates to 'not prop_0 implies prop_1 holds until prop_0.' In LTL, this is written using the implication and 'until' operator.

This specification requires that prop_1 holds in every state before the first state where prop_0 holds, and imposes no restriction after prop_0 occurs. In LTL, 'before' is often encoded using the release operator, where !(prop_0) releases prop_1: prop_1 must hold until prop_0 becomes true; if prop_0 never becomes true, prop_1 must hold forever.

The requirement states that before 'prop_0' becomes true, 'prop_1' must always hold. In LTL, this can be expressed as 'For all states before the first occurrence of prop_0, prop_1 holds'. This is captured by '(not prop_0) U (prop_0 & G(prop_1))', but since we only care about before prop_0, it is commonly written as '((not prop_0) -> prop_1) U prop_0'.

The requirement 'Before prop_0, the controller shall prop_1.' means that prop_1 must be true at every point in time before prop_0 becomes true. This translates to: it is always the case that if prop_0 has not yet occurred, then prop_1 must be true. Or equivalently, before prop_0, prop_1 always holds. This is expressed as: 'not prop_0' releases 'prop_1'.

The formula states that before prop_0 becomes true for the first time, prop_1 must hold at every step. That is, up until (but not including) the first point where prop_0 is true, prop_1 must be true. This can be represented using the 'until' (U) operator, by saying: either prop_0 never happens (so prop_1 is always true), or up until the first occurrence of prop_0, prop_1 must be true.
